picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA reported record Q1 FY2027 revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY), beat EPS by $0.11, and guided Q2 to $91B — more than $4B above consensus — while maintaining 75% gross margins. The Blackwell architecture is in full production ramp and HBM supply chain is locked. At ~$194 today (off the 52-week high of $236.54), NVDA has pulled back ~18% from peak, offering a better entry relative to recent weeks while the secular AI capex supercycle — with hyperscalers collectively spending nearly $800B in 2026 — remains fully intact. Tepper nearly doubled his AMZN stake as AWS proxy; NVDA is the direct picks-and-shovels beneficiary of all that capex.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings report (August) with $91B guidance validation and Blackwell gross-margin progression toward 77-78%
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$194, ~18% below 52-week high of $236.54; P/E of ~30x is elevated but supported by 85% revenue growth. Consolidating above the 50dma after July 1 tech selloff — constructive re-entry zone.
picks.exitTrigger: Q2 gross margin falls materially below 73%, or any hyperscaler (Microsoft, Google, Meta, Amazon) announces a capex reduction; China export controls expand to cover additional NVDA products
picks.keyRisk: Multiple compression if AI capex cycle peaks earlier than expected or custom ASIC hyperscaler alternatives (Google TPUs, Amazon Trainium, Meta MTIA) accelerate market share gains

picks.name: Meta Platforms, Inc.
picks.rationale: META delivered its fastest revenue growth quarter since 2021 in Q1 2026 (+33% YoY to $56.3B), beat EPS by 7.7%, and raised capex guidance to $125-145B — demonstrating that AI-driven ad monetization is compounding even as the company invests aggressively. The 43% operating margin confirms AI investment is not structurally degrading profitability. Sector rotation data shows META in the 'Lagging' momentum quadrant — a technical headwind — but fundamentals are strongest-in-class for the platform economy, and Ackman added MSFT as a co-conviction, not an exit from the AI/platform thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — midpoint guidance of $59.5B revenue (+25% YoY) with any upside confirmation on AI ad-load monetization and Llama/AI agent progress
picks.entryNote: Stock pulled back from highs on capex concern; trades at ~17-18x forward earnings — inexpensive for 25%+ revenue growth and 43% operating margins. Down ~13% YTD at one point, providing a reasonable mean-reversion entry vs. the fundamental trajectory.
picks.exitTrigger: Operating margins fall below 38% on capex overspend without revenue acceleration, or digital advertising market deteriorates materially (recession signal), or regulatory breakup action advances concretely
picks.keyRisk: $125-145B capex commitment poses FCF compression risk if AI ad monetization timelines slip beyond 2026-2027

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO has beaten estimates on sales and adjusted EPS in each of its last four quarters, with Q2 FY2026 projections of 47% YoY revenue growth and 52% EPS growth — and a $73B custom AI chip backlog from confirmed hyperscaler customers including Google, Meta, ByteDance, OpenAI, and a fifth unnamed major player. Operating margins above 60% are exceptional even for premium semis, and the stock is modestly outperforming NVDA YTD at ~+20%. Druckenmiller initiated AVGO as his largest Q1 purchase — a powerful smart money confirmation of the custom ASIC thesis as the AI infrastructure value chain diversifies.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report (September) validating the $73B custom chip backlog conversion and AI revenue run rate toward $15B+ quarterly
picks.entryNote: Off post-earnings volatility (range of -11% to +9% in last 4 prints); trading at a discount to intrinsic value on cash flow basis per analyst consensus. Highest-quality risk-adjusted entry in the AI semiconductor space relative to NVDA's extension.
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of any top-2 hyperscaler custom chip design win, AI ASIC revenue below $10B per quarter, or operating margins compress below 55%
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration risk — a single hyperscaler deciding to consolidate custom chip spend or build in-house capability would materially impair the $73B backlog

picks.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
picks.rationale: MU posted the strongest all-factor quarter in the screen universe: +22.6% EPS beat ($25.11 vs. $20.49 estimated), +16.2% revenue beat ($41.46B vs. $35.84B), Q4 guidance of ~$50B crushed the $43.58B consensus, gross margins hit a record 84.9%, and 15 analysts immediately revised estimates upward. HBM3E and HBM4 are fully booked through 2027 with demand extending into 2028, and record FCF was achieved in Q3 with Q4 projected above $30B. Druckenmiller initiated a position across memory names (SanDisk, Seagate, Micron) in Q1 as part of a deliberate memory-cycle inflection bet.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (September) — guiding toward ~$50B revenue; any confirmation of HBM4 pricing power and gross margin sustainability above 80% would be a major re-rating event
picks.entryNote: Stock is extended after a 285% run from its March lows, and RSI is approaching overbought — this warrants disciplined sizing rather than avoidance; the fundamental setup is best-in-universe. Carry-over from prior week with weight discipline maintained.
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margins revert below 75%, HBM pricing softens materially on competitor supply entry (Samsung, SK Hynix capacity unlock), or hyperscaler capex freeze reduces HBM demand
picks.keyRisk: Parabolic price extension creates significant drawdown vulnerability on any demand disappointment even if the multi-year thesis remains intact

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL beat revenue consensus in Q1 2026 across all four major reporting mega-caps; Berkshire Hathaway under Greg Abel added +224% to its GOOGL position making it the #7 holding at ~$16.6B — the most decisive institutional endorsement of Alphabet's AI/Cloud trajectory in the current cycle. Google Cloud's expanding margins and the TPU co-design relationship with Broadcom provide durable AI infrastructure monetization. Trading at ~17x forward earnings, GOOGL is the cheapest of the hyperscalers on a growth-adjusted basis and represents an increasingly rare combination of value and momentum in the mega-cap technology universe.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Google Cloud margin expansion, Search AI monetization (AI Overviews ad load), and YouTube ad revenue re-acceleration
picks.entryNote: Berkshire added +224% during Q1 2026 — one of the largest institutional conviction additions of the cycle. Stock consolidating after Q1 beat; technical base intact. Most attractively valued mega-cap tech name.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Search market share falls below 85% due to AI chatbot disintermediation, Google Cloud margins stagnate below 12%, or DOJ antitrust remedy forces structural divestiture of Search
picks.keyRisk: AI chatbot search disintermediation (OpenAI/Microsoft Copilot) reducing Search query volume and CPM rates, threatening the core $175B/year advertising franchise

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI completed the spin-off of its Mobility division on July 1, 2026 — a clean catalyst that sharpens the story to pure-play financial intelligence (Ratings, Market Intelligence, Commodity Insights, Indices). Post-spin, capital allocation pivots immediately to buybacks, creating an earnings-per-share accretion tailwind. With 24 analysts rating it 'Strong Buy' and a consensus 12-month target of $532.81 (+35% upside), the market hasn't fully priced the post-Mobility clarity. Ratings revenue will benefit from any M&A and debt issuance rebound in a higher-rate environment where companies need to refinance, and the Indices business is a perpetual toll-road on AUM growth.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings post-spin (late July/August) — first clean read on post-Mobility organic growth rate for core Ratings and Indices, plus buyback cadence announcement
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4-8 weeks (Q2 earnings + buyback cadence disclosure)
picks.entryNote: Rothschild lowered PT to $520 (still a Buy), giving a modestly more conservative target to anchor against. Stock is in price discovery post-spin with consensus at $532.81 — the near-term overhang of the spin is now resolved, offering a cleaner entry.
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance/M&A markets freeze (recession-driven), or ratings revenue declines more than 15% YoY on deal flow collapse; or if buyback program is smaller than $3B annually
picks.keyRisk: A recession-driven collapse in corporate debt issuance and M&A activity would severely impair the Ratings segment, which is SPGI's highest-margin revenue stream

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX sits at the convergence of two structural supercycles: NATO re-armament (confirmed spending commitments post-Ukraine) and commercial aerospace aftermarket recovery. The defense segment benefits directly from geopolitical re-armament as Middle East tensions and European defense buildout drive multi-year backlog expansion, while the Pratt & Whitney GTF engine aftermarket is a high-margin recurring revenue stream. Sector rotation data confirms Industrials/Defense as a Tier 1 co-leader through July, with the 'real economy' rotation intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — backlog update across defense/aerospace, Pratt & Whitney GTF engine replacement program progress, and updated 2026 free cash flow guidance
picks.entryNote: Carry-over from prior 7 trading days with consistent high-conviction ranking. Trading at fair value vs. defense peers; backlog provides earnings visibility over 3-5 year horizon. Defense names have historically held up well in late-cycle/stagflationary regimes.
picks.exitTrigger: US defense budget sequestration cuts RTX program funding by more than 10%, GTF engine durability problems escalate beyond manageable warranty costs, or M&A integration issues emerge
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF engine powder metal disk issue escalation — any new material safety finding would generate earnings headwind and reputational damage with airlines

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM completed its legal domicile shift to Texas on July 1, 2026 — a shareholder-friendly governance signal — while trading at $137-138 with a 29% 1-year return and a DCF-implied intrinsic value of ~$274/share (a 49.8% discount per the model). Energy is confirmed as the #1 sector by relative strength as of June 30 with 22%+ YTD gains, Middle East tensions sustain geopolitical premium, and XOM's structural AI power demand thesis (data centers require enormous energy) provides a secular demand floor independent of oil price volatility. FCF is projected to reach $47B by 2030 on analyst estimates.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — Permian production volume update, Pioneer integration synergy progress, and LNG project milestone confirmations
picks.entryNote: XOM pulled back ~10-16% over 30-90 days on oil price volatility — a healthy consolidation within a structural uptrend (+29% 1-year). Average analyst target of $165.55 represents 20%+ upside from current levels. Valuation and FCF yield are compelling relative to history.
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude falls sustainably below $65/barrel, Hormuz blockade fully resolves and geopolitical premium collapses, or Pioneer integration generates material operational write-downs
picks.keyRisk: Oil price decline driven by demand destruction (recession) or a rapid Middle East peace resolution removing the geopolitical premium

picks.name: Quanta Services, Inc.
picks.rationale: PWR is the dominant US grid infrastructure contractor and is a direct, high-conviction beneficiary of the AI data center power buildout — a decade-long capex cycle. With every hyperscaler committing to massive data center expansions requiring new transmission and grid connection infrastructure, Quanta's order book is expanding structurally. The AI power demand narrative is a secular override above macro cycle concerns, and PWR has been a consistent top-10 holding for 7 consecutive trading days in our prior book. Industrials remain a confirmed Tier 1 sector co-leader through the real economy rotation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — backlog magnitude update, data center power infrastructure contract announcements, and 2026/2027 revenue guidance raise
picks.entryNote: Consistent carry-over with thesis fully intact. PWR trades at a premium to general industrial peers given the visibility of the AI-driven grid buildout cycle — premium is justified by backlog duration and contract certainty. Structural trends in power demand and electrification are likely to drive long-term investment for years.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler data center capex freeze reduces grid buildout orders materially, or union labor cost inflation compresses margins below 10% EBIT, or federal energy permitting reform stalls
picks.keyRisk: Permitting delays for new transmission lines — a chronic bottleneck in US grid expansion — could slow revenue recognition even as the backlog grows

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the leading competitive power generator in the US and sits at the nexus of the AI data center electricity demand supercycle — data centers require 24/7 baseload power that only nuclear and natural gas can reliably provide. VST's nuclear fleet (via the Comanche Peak and Beaver Valley acquisitions) provides near-zero marginal cost electricity that commands a premium in AI power purchase agreements. The stock entered the top-10 book on July 1 as a new addition and the thesis has strengthened: energy sector is confirmed #1 by relative strength, and power generators are the most direct beneficiary of AI electricity demand without the AI-valuation-multiple risk.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — data center PPA (power purchase agreement) announcement progress, nuclear capacity factor update, and 2026-2027 EBITDA guidance confirmation
picks.entryNote: New addition as of July 1; first full-conviction day in the book. Trading at a discount to the value of its power contracts and nuclear assets as the market is still pricing it as a traditional utility. The AI-driven re-rating of baseload power generators is in early innings.
picks.exitTrigger: AI data center power demand growth slows materially, nuclear plant regulatory issues force capacity deratings, or natural gas price spike compresses generation margins
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory risk on nuclear plant license extensions — any NRC adverse ruling on VST's nuclear facilities would permanently impair the baseload power thesis

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD is the most direct hedge against all three macro tail risks simultaneously: inflation re-acceleration (CPI at 4.2% and rising), Middle East geopolitical escalation (Hormuz risk still live), and a potential policy error. With the real policy rate barely positive, the opportunity cost of holding gold has collapsed. Gold's low beta (~0.51) provides genuine portfolio volatility dampening. Starboard Value added 11% to its IAU gold ETF position in Q1 2026, joining a pattern of institutional defensive hedging.
picks.catalyst: June CPI print (July 14) — a hot print above 4.5% would spike gold as a stagflation hedge; any Hormuz re-escalation provides immediate upside
picks.entryNote: Gold at or near all-time highs reflects genuine monetary and geopolitical demand — not speculative bubble. Hold as regime insurance, not momentum trade. Size at 6% of book.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed pivots decisively dovish (cuts rates 3+ times rapidly), Middle East conflict fully resolves with normalized Hormuz transit, and CPI falls sustainably below 3%
picks.keyRisk: A rapid, aggressive Fed tightening cycle that pushes real rates sharply positive (above +200bps) would be the primary headwind for gold

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD surged 19% YTD and 26% over the past year, far outpacing the S&P 500's 7% gain as investors rotate into defensive dividend income — a direct validation of the late-cycle rotation thesis. Holdings span consumer defensives (~19%), healthcare (~19%), energy (~17%), and industrials (~11%), providing broad defensive diversification in one vehicle. The March 2026 reconstitution upgraded toward higher dividend growth stocks. The ADP jobs miss on July 1 (only 98K private jobs added) reinforces the consumer strain thesis that makes dividend compounders more attractive.
picks.catalyst: September 2026 dividend distribution announcement — any maintained or increased distribution would validate the dividend growth narrative against the macro headwind
picks.entryNote: Confirmed 26% 1-year return — this is not a stagnant defensive; it is actively working as a rotation destination. Entry here is buying proven late-cycle leadership, not chasing. Carry-over from prior 7 trading days.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks above 4.75% sustainably (pressures dividend equity valuations), or two consecutive quarterly dividend distributions decline YoY signaling dividend growth thesis breakdown
picks.keyRisk: A sustained move in the 10-year Treasury above 4.75% would reprice dividend equity valuations, replicating the 2022 rate-driven selloff in income stocks

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B holds $397.4B in cash and T-bills (earning ~3.6% risk-free), trades at ~1.5x book (historically a floor), and operates businesses across insurance, rails, energy, and consumer that are structurally uncorrelated to AI narratives. Greg Abel's first major portfolio renovation added GOOGL (+224%) and DAL while hoarding cash — a clear signal that valuations are too rich for aggressive deployment, providing embedded downside protection. Insurance float earnings benefit directly from the higher-for-longer rate environment. Near-zero correlation to Nasdaq drawdowns makes BRK.B the ideal defensive ballast in a concentrated growth book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — cash deployment update (any new acquisition signal would be a major catalyst), operating earnings trajectory from insurance underwriting
picks.entryNote: Carry-over from prior 7 trading days. Trades at ~1.5x book — historically a floor. The $397B cash pile is optionality at no cost: it earns yield while waiting for distressed assets. No AI multiple risk embedded.
picks.exitTrigger: Berkshire deploys cash aggressively into overvalued assets (destroys the value discipline thesis), or insurance underwriting results deteriorate materially from catastrophe exposure
picks.keyRisk: A prolonged low-volatility environment with no market dislocations eliminates the deployment catalyst, making the cash pile a drag on relative performance vs. risk assets

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides ~4.4% current yield with far less duration risk than TLT in a scenario where long yields could re-accelerate toward 5%+ on fiscal concerns. At the 10Y at 4.37%, IEF captures meaningful real yield while providing recession-scenario optionality: if the economy slows faster than expected, the Fed pivots and IEF appreciates. Bonds are likely to be an effective hedge against the largest equity risks (recession or AI optimism reversal). This is clean rate-cut optionality without TLT's catastrophic convexity risk in a fiscal-pressure scenario.
picks.catalyst: July 14 June CPI print — a soft CPI below 3.8% would immediately price in reduced hike probability and drive IEF appreciation; any economic slowdown signal reinforces the recession hedge
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2 weeks (July 14 CPI), then July 28-29 FOMC
picks.entryNote: 10Y at 4.37% — near the upper end of the 2026 range. Medium conviction given the Fed's hike bias makes duration a two-way trade. Position sized at 4% as a hedge instrument, not a return driver. Carry-over from prior 7 trading days.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year yield breaks above 5.0% decisively on fiscal deterioration or another hot CPI, at which point duration should be cut further; or Fed hikes 50bp in a single meeting (shock scenario)
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal deterioration (large Treasury supply from deficit financing) pushes the term premium sharply higher, driving the 10Y yield above 5% and generating mark-to-market losses

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the canonical late-cycle consumer staples compounder — a global beverage duopoly with 60+ years of consecutive dividend growth, genuine pricing power in an inflationary environment (KO has demonstrated the ability to pass through cost inflation), and revenues that are structurally insulated from US economic cycles (70%+ of revenue is international). In a stagflationary regime where consumer spending is strained by negative real wage growth, KO's non-discretionary demand profile provides true defensive income. The SCHD ETF in this sleeve already holds KO at 4.1% — confirming the thesis across managers.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth rate vs. volume/price mix, and international market performance across EM currencies
picks.entryNote: Consistent carry-over from prior 5+ trading days. Trades at a modest premium to historical forward P/E but justified by the quality and duration of its global franchise. Dividend yield of ~2.8% provides floor returns in a risk-off scenario.
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth falls below 3% on volume declines (suggesting pricing power exhaustion), or a material acceleration in GLP-1 drug adoption reduces global beverage consumption structurally over 3-5 years
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 weight-loss drug adoption accelerating faster than expected, reducing per-capita beverage consumption globally and impacting long-term volume growth

summary: The portfolio maintains a Cautiously Bullish stance in a confirmed late-cycle / stagflationary regime: the Fed is on hold at 3.50-3.75% with a hike bias, core PCE surprised at 3.8% YoY in May (above the FOMC's own 3.3% projection), and the next live policy catalyst is the July 14 CPI print followed by the July 28-29 FOMC. The 10 growth picks express three high-conviction secular themes: (1) AI infrastructure dominance — NVDA, AVGO, MU as the semiconductor picks-and-shovels with complementary value-chain exposure; (2) the AI-enabler / real-economy rotation — GOOGL and META as hyperscaler platforms with durable monetization, RTX and PWR as industrial beneficiaries of defense re-armament and grid buildout, and VST as the direct AI power demand play; and (3) financial intelligence compounding — SPGI post-Mobility-spin and XOM as an energy value-plus-geopolitical-hedge name. All 10 carry-over from recent sessions with thesis continuity intact; VST was the only new addition (introduced July 1), replacing GS, which was rotated out as the Financials stress-test catalyst is now fully priced. The 5 defensive picks (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) are sized collectively at 25% of the book and provide genuine regime insurance against the dominant tail risk being underwritten: a hot June CPI on July 14 that forces markets to fully price a September hike, creating simultaneous compression in long-duration growth, credit, and rate-sensitive equities.
diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors — Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, MU: 3 names, the hard maximum), Communication Services (META, GOOGL), Industrials (RTX, PWR), Energy (XOM), and Utilities (VST) — with no more than 3 names sharing any single end-market; NVDA and AVGO share AI chip exposure but differ materially (GPU infrastructure vs. custom ASIC/networking), while MU is differentiated as the memory/storage bottleneck play. Defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold inflation/geopolitical hedge (GLD), broad dividend-quality income (SCHD), value-compounding cash fortress (BRK.B), and intermediate-duration rate-cut optionality (IEF), plus consumer staples income (KO) — zero overlap across the five instruments.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA sits at the apex of the AI infrastructure supercycle with FY2026 revenue of $215.94B (+65% YoY) and $120B in earnings — exceptional fundamentals supported by the Blackwell and Vera Rubin architecture ramp. The macro briefing confirms hyperscaler capex approaching $800B in 2026, a direct order-book tailwind for NVDA's data center segment. Today's ~$195 price sits ~17% below its all-time high of $236.54 reached May 14, and 62 analysts carry a consensus $298.93 target (+52% upside), making this a buy-the-dip entry within an intact secular trend. Persistent top-holding in this book for 7 consecutive sessions — thesis unchanged.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings report featuring Vera Rubin ramp revenue disclosure and updated hyperscaler order commitments
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$195 vs. 52-week range $151–$237; ~17% off ATH, consolidating above $192 support; EPS TTM $6.53 implies ~30x trailing — elevated but justified by 65%+ revenue growth; consensus 12M target $298.93
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 70%, confirmed hyperscaler capex pause or material in-house chip substitution (AMZN Trainium, GOOGL TPU) displacing Blackwell orders
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex rationalization or export control escalation interrupting China demand and global order flow

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta has been a top-4 holding in this book for 7 consecutive sessions — thesis fully intact. The company is a direct AI monetization compounder: advertising revenue is accelerating on AI-personalization gains, Llama 4 models underpin platform efficiency, and Reality Labs losses are stabilizing. The sector rotation scorecard flags Communication Services as 'coiling' with ad markets improving, while META's FCF generation, share buyback velocity, and expanding AI-driven ad CPMs make it the cleanest quality compounder in the XLC space. Core PCE at 3.4% and resilient payrolls (+172K May) support sustained advertiser spending, the primary revenue driver.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with AI-driven ad revenue growth and Llama monetization update
picks.entryNote: Trading near multi-month highs following consolidation; P/E ~27x on accelerating EPS trajectory; ad-market tailwinds from resilient labor market (unemployment 4.3%) underpin near-term estimates
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue growth decelerating below 15% YoY or Llama competitive moat eroding to closed-source rivals (GPT-5/Claude 4) on enterprise adoption
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory antitrust action on Instagram/WhatsApp integration or AI content liability legislation

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire Hathaway's Greg Abel added ~40M shares to bring total to ~57.8M shares worth ~$16.6B in Q1 2026 — the most consequential smart-money endorsement of the quarter. GOOGL trades at ~26x P/E (cheapest Mag-7), while Google Cloud is approaching 20%+ operating margins and Search monetization continues to prove structurally resilient. The Apple-Google Gemini AI deal announced January 2026 embeds Alphabet's AI directly into the world's largest device ecosystem, unlocking incremental monetization not yet in consensus estimates. Continuous top-3 or top-5 holding for 7 sessions; thesis strengthened by today's analyst commentary on AI coding investments.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with Google Cloud margin progression and Gemini AI revenue attach rate disclosure
picks.entryNote: GOOGL at $339 vs. 52-week range $170–$409; P/E ~26x vs. Mag-7 average ~35x — structural discount for world-class ROIC >30% and $70B+ TTM FCF; Berkshire's 57.8M share position provides institutional floor
picks.exitTrigger: Search revenue share declining materially to AI-native competitors (Perplexity, OpenAI) or DOJ antitrust remedy forcing Search divestiture
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust ruling requiring structural separation of Google Search from Chrome/Android distribution

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's $30B AI chip booking backlog and newly unveiled Jalapeño AI chip collaboration with OpenAI create a multi-year custom silicon revenue ramp that is structurally distinct from NVDA's GPU dominance — complementary, not correlated. The VMware integration continues generating >70% infrastructure software margins, creating a rare dual-engine compounder. Druckenmiller's Duquesne allocated $60.6M to AVGO in Q1 2026, adding institutional credibility. The macro backdrop of accelerating hyperscaler capex ($800B estimated by BofA) directly feeds Broadcom's networking ASICs and custom chip pipeline across Google, Meta, and Apple.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings with AI revenue segment disclosure and Jalapeño chip production timeline update
picks.entryNote: Trading with consistent upward EPS revisions post-VMware; AI backlog of $30B provides multi-quarter revenue visibility; infrastructure software recurring revenue >70% margin acts as valuation floor
picks.exitTrigger: AI chip backlog cancellations or VMware customer churn exceeding 15% as renewal cycles complete; loss of Google or Meta custom ASIC design wins
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration in hyperscaler custom ASIC contracts — loss of one major design partnership (Apple, Google, or Meta) would materially impair the AI revenue ramp thesis

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs has been a top-5 to top-7 holding in this book for all 7 prior sessions with a consistently intact thesis. The yield curve re-steepening to +27 bps (2s10s) directly expands net interest margins while the rebuilding M&A pipeline (macro context: strategic urgency driving deal activity across biopharma and tech) accelerates advisory fee revenue. As the premier capital markets franchise, GS benefits asymmetrically from both the AI-era IPO cycle and the defensive M&A consolidation wave. The sector scorecard flags capital markets as the preferred Financial sub-sector over regional banks, and GS is the blue-chip expression of that view.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with M&A advisory and equity underwriting revenue disclosure — pipeline rebuild confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading at fair-to-cheap vs. 10-year median P/B; curve steepening (+27 bps 2s10s) is a direct NIM catalyst not yet fully reflected in consensus; institutional accumulation steady per OBV trend
picks.exitTrigger: M&A pipeline stalling on macro deterioration, credit quality deterioration in lending book (HY OAS widening above 350 bps), or regulatory capital surcharge increase
picks.keyRisk: Macro growth shock causing M&A freeze and equity underwriting collapse simultaneously — the primary risk to the dual-revenue-driver thesis

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI has been a top-5 to top-8 holding for 7 consecutive sessions — the thesis is a durable compounder in data, analytics, and ratings that benefits from both elevated issuance activity (corporates refinancing at higher yields) and the secular data monetization trend. The Q1 2026 filing confirmed a $0.97 quarterly dividend increase and strong Market Intelligence operating profit. Elevated rates drive credit issuance volumes as companies are forced to roll maturing debt, directly feeding Ratings revenue. The Mobility segment spin creates a clean-story catalyst as SPGI refocuses on its highest-margin divisions.
picks.catalyst: Mobility segment spin completion and Q2 2026 earnings with revised guidance for remaining core segments
picks.catalystWindow: Q3 2026 (spin expected completion and guidance update)
picks.entryNote: SPGI trades at a premium but justified by 90%+ subscription revenue, near-monopoly Ratings economics, and consistently upward EPS revisions; Mobility spin expected to unlock sum-of-parts value
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapsing in a recession (credit markets seizing), or DOJ antitrust action on ratings duopoly
picks.keyRisk: Recession-driven credit market freeze halting bond issuance and crushing Ratings revenue — SPGI's highest-margin, most cyclically sensitive segment

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX has been in the top-7 of this book for all 7 prior sessions reflecting the durable defense supercycle thesis. The geopolitical backdrop — Strait of Hormuz tensions resurging (cargo ship attack near Oman this week), Iranian ceasefire fragility, and NATO re-armament — sustains multi-year defense budget expansion. RTX's Pratt & Whitney engine business has a structural backlog from commercial aviation recovery, and Raytheon missile systems (Patriot, AMRAAM) are in structural deficit vs. global demand. The industrials sector scorecard rates defense as a consistent leader with positive earnings breadth and the macro confirms 'Defense: geopolitical re-armament cycle' as a working regime trade.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with defense backlog update and Pratt & Whitney GTF engine settlement progress
picks.entryNote: Trading at fair-to-rich vs. history but defense order books insulate from macro softness; GTF engine fix costs are progressively de-risking; NATO spending commitments locking in multi-year revenue
picks.exitTrigger: Iran peace deal becoming fully durable (30%+ oil price collapse reducing defense budget pressure), or GTF engine liability costs re-escalating beyond provisioned amounts

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.

Here is your full macro briefing as of **Friday, June 26, 2026**:

---

## 📊 US EQUITY REGIME BRIEFING — SENIOR MACRO STRATEGY
*Prepared for: Long-Only Portfolio Manager | Date: June 26, 2026*

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**


The Fed held the federal funds rate unchanged at **3.50%–3.75%** for a fourth consecutive meeting in June 2026.
 
June 17, 2026 was the first meeting under new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh.
 
The updated dot plot reflects a hawkish pivot, with a **3.8% median rate projection for 2026**.
 
For the end of 2026, most officials now expect the benchmark rate to sit between **3.6% and 4.1%** (up from their previous estimate of 3.25%–3.75%).
 
Fed rate expectations remain elevated, with **markets pricing in ~3 rate hikes** this year, and the probability of the first move in September standing at around **62%**.
 
The longer-run neutral rate anchor has a heavy concentration of participants pinning it exactly at **3.0%**
 — meaning the current policy rate at 3.625% midpoint sits approximately **+62 bps above neutral**, a meaningfully restrictive real posture given headline PCE already at 4.1%.

**YIELD CURVE**


As of market close June 25, 2026, the US Treasury yield curve is upward-sloping: **2Y = 4.13%**, **10Y = 4.40%**, **30Y = 4.86%**.
 
The **2s10s spread = +27 bps** and the **3m10y spread = +62 bps**.
 Both are firmly positive after a prolonged inversion. 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support longer maturities, aligning with the view that benchmark yields stay higher for longer.


**INFLATION**


May CPI came in at **4.2% YoY**, up from 3.8% in April — the **third straight month of accelerating headline inflation**.
 
Energy is most of the heat: the conflict with Iran and disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz pushed energy prices up **23.5%** over the past year.
 
Core CPI, however, rose just **0.2% MoM** — half of April's pace — with its annual rate at **2.9%**.
 
The May PCE print showed **headline PCE at 4.1%** and **core PCE at 3.4%** — the highest core since 2023 and well above the Fed's 2% target, though both matched forecasts.
 Regime characterization: **energy-driven re-acceleration in headline, sticky-but-contained core** — a bifurcated inflation picture that is politically difficult for the Fed to look through.


Fed officials revised their median core PCE forecast for 2026 to **3.3%** (vs. 2.7% in March), and headline PCE to **3.6%**.
 
This reflects a consensus that the downward trajectory of inflation in this cycle will be slower than previously anticipated.


**GROWTH**


GDP growth for 2026 is now projected at **2.2%** (down from 2.4% in March).
 
May payrolls came in at **+172K** vs. an +80K consensus; revisions added **+93K** to prior two months, with unemployment holding at **4.3%**.
 
The FOMC statement noted economic activity is "expanding at a solid pace" with "productivity growth and capital investment strong."
 
Wages are the one clear soft spot, with annual growth easing to **3.4%**.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**


**HY OAS = ~275 bps** (as of early June 2026)
 — 
down ~14% over the past year and well below the long-term average of 393 bps and the historical median of 452 bps.
 
Broad **IG OAS is approximately 80 bps** — near 25-year tights.
 Credit is screaming risk-on, but at these levels leaves little cushion for spread widening if growth softens. 
The 10Y yield is **4.39%** on the week — down 7 bps.


**EARNINGS & VALUATION**


The S&P 500 trailing earnings yield stands at approximately **3.98%** as of June 23, 2026
, implying a trailing P/E of roughly **~25x**. 
With the 10Y yield at 4.40%, the earnings yield is **below** Treasury yields, suggesting stocks are not cheap relative to bonds by historical standards.
 
The yield gap (earnings yield minus 10Y Treasury) is currently approximately **–0.37%**, about 0.30 standard deviations below the historical average — indicating equity markets are near fairly valued but offer no margin of safety vs. fixed income.


**GEOPOLITICAL TAPE**


The US and Iran have agreed to a provisional peace deal, though oil prices remain above pre-war levels, while recent labor market data has pointed to continued employment strength.
 
Geopolitical tensions resurfaced this week following an attack on a cargo ship near Oman in the Strait of Hormuz.
 
The US and Iran signaled progress in talks after their memorandum of understanding halted the naval blockade, and tanker flows through the Strait of Hormuz have picked up.


---

## ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-Cycle, Elevated-Inflation Variant.** This is a late-cycle regime defined by the simultaneous presence of above-neutral real policy rates (FFR midpoint ~3.625% vs. neutral 3.0%), re-accelerating headline inflation driven by a geopolitical energy supply shock (headline PCE 4.1%, headline CPI 4.2% YoY), 
a hawkish dot-plot pivot toward a 3.8% median 2026 rate with policymakers raising inflation forecasts significantly and expecting to keep rates high for a prolonged period
, and a labor market that remains resilient but is no longer the source of wage-push pressure (
wages decelerating to 3.4% YoY
). The curve has re-steepened positively (2s10s +27 bps, 3m10y +62 bps) — historically this signals early recovery *or* fiscal/term premium stress; in this context, given HY OAS near multi-decade tights and equities near full valuation, it reflects the latter. The bull case (mid-cycle extension) rests entirely on the Iran ceasefire holding and energy disinflationary spillover into core — the base case is that the Fed is boxed in, policy easing is deferred well into 2027, and the cycle clock is ticking.

---

## ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** Energy & Commodities (direct beneficiary of Strait of Hormuz premium; energy up 23.5% YoY), Defense (geopolitical re-armament cycle), Quality Large-Cap Tech with AI revenue — 
capital investment is strong
 and AI infrastructure capex continues to support Mega-Cap earnings. Financials benefit from a steepening curve (2s10s back to +27 bps from deep inversion) expanding net interest margins. Short-duration, high-quality IG bonds now offer competitive real returns. **Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors are under pressure — homebuilders, REITs, and small-cap growth are impaired by higher-for-longer rates. 
Inflation has proven sticky over the past few years, with both CPI and PCE still well above the Fed's 2% target
 — this kills the "soft landing normalizes policy" narrative that powered the 2024–25 expansion trade. 
Markets are pricing an 80% chance of a Fed rate hike by December
, which is deeply hostile to long-duration equities and speculative/unprofitable growth names. Consumer discretionary faces a squeeze from persistent energy/food price inflation eroding real spending power.

---

## ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH

**1. July ISM Manufacturing & Services (July 1 & 3) + June Jobs Report (July 3):** A payroll print above +150K with another strong revision confirms the "no landing" scenario and makes an October Fed hike near-certainty. A miss (sub-100K) would crack the thesis and is the highest-probability near-term catalyst for a meaningful rates rally and equity rotation from defensives back to growth.

**2. Strait of Hormuz / Iran Deal Durability:** 
Geopolitical tensions resurfaced following an attack on a cargo ship near Oman in the Strait of Hormuz.
 Energy prices are the single most important variable governing headline PCE, the Fed's optionality, and consumer confidence. A re-escalation pushing oil back above $90–$95/bbl would materially accelerate the case for Fed hikes and compress multiples. Any durable ceasefire and tanker flow normalization is the most powerful disinflationary force available — it's the bull market's clearest green light.

**3. June CPI (July 11) & Core PCE (July 25):** 
The Fed raised short-term inflation forecasts significantly, indicating that the current pace of cooling prices is slower than optimistic projections.
 These two prints are the Fed's permission slip. A second consecutive month of 0.2% MoM core CPI — as seen in May — combined with lower energy prices would de-price the hike cycle, compress real yields, and re-open the equity multiple expansion trade. A hot print (0.3%+ MoM core) validates the hawkish dot plot and likely precipitates spread widening in HY (currently at historically narrow 275 bps with little buffer) and a re-test of equity lows.

---
*Data as of June 26, 2026. Sources: FOMC June 2026 SEP, US Treasury, BLS, BEA, ICE BofA / FRED, GuruFocus, TradingEconomics.*

A bear case with continued share loss to Chinese EVs and robotaxi delays takes Tesla back to the $180–220 range

**The core problem:** World-class growth but priced at multiples that have historically ended in catastrophic drawdowns.

A 20-year study of over 70 software stocks that pushed their price-to-sales ratio above 100x found only seven names that ever joined that club. All seven eventually fell at least 65% from peak, with an average drawdown near 79%.
 For comparison: 
Snowflake dropped around 73% after peaking at 222x sales, while Zoom cratered about 90% from a 123x sales apex.

Despite Q1 2026 revenue surging 85% YoY to $1.63B, valuation concerns and reports of insider selling weighed on sentiment.

- **Earnings cliff.** 
Analysts forecast Q2 EPS of $3.75–$4.75, down 25% from last year's $6.80.


**Risk factors: DOJ criminal conviction, Medicare Advantage reform, antitrust forced restructuring, earnings estimate resets, CEO vacuum.**

### 🔴 4. SNOW — Snowflake | *Consumption Slowdown + Competition in a Crowded Cloud*

- **Structural multiple problem.** Despite a post-AI bounce, SNOW's consumption model is inherently volatile. 
Risk factors include customers continuing to rationalize budgets and prioritize cash flow management, including through shortened contract durations, and the extent to which customers continue to optimize consumption.


- **Competitive pressure is intensifying.** Databricks (private), BigQuery (GOOGL), and Amazon Redshift are attacking SNOW's core. Meanwhile, the AI data cloud narrative requires constant execution to justify premium pricing.

- **Historical precedent.** As noted above, 
Snowflake dropped around 73% after peaking at 222x sales.
 The current multiple, while compressed from peak, still prices perfection in an environment where customer budget scrutiny has increased.

- **Analyst caution.** 
Snowflake is a company whose stock some analysts are currently avoiding
 given elevated valuation despite slower growth relative to peers like Palantir.

**Risk factors: Consumption-based revenue model volatility, budget rationalization, Microsoft/Google competitive pressure, gross margin pressure as AI credits are priced aggressively.**

 Fundamentals remain exceptional.
